title: Why Apple Sued OpenAI, New York Takes on Data Centers, and What to Know about Cyclosporiasis
gist: Uncanny Valley podcast transcript covers Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI over alleged theft of hardware secrets via 400+ former Apple hires, a new OpenAI-employee-funded super PAC ($5M, targeting $15M) opposing Greg Brockman's $100M pro-AI 'Leading the Future' fund, DOGE's unaccountable use of AI to shape HUD housing/contract decisions, New York's first statewide data center moratorium and Trump's public attack on it, and the record 2026 cyclosporiasis outbreak linked to lettuce amid CDC staffing cuts.

insights.claim: Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI alleges the company's chief hardware officer, 24-year Apple veteran Tang Tan, encouraged departing Apple employees to bring proprietary information and unreleased technology with them, and that OpenAI has hired more than 400 former Apple employees.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Signals real IP/talent bleed from Apple to OpenAI's hardware unit; discovery could surface more, raising litigation and reputational risk for OpenAI's hardware roadmap.
insights.quote: OpenAI has hired more than 400 former Apple employees according to the lawsuit.
insights.locator: Just to ground what we're talking about a little bit more

insights.claim: OpenAI paid $6.5 billion last year to acquire IO Products, a hardware startup co-founded by former Apple executives including Tang Tan, Scott Cannon, Evans Hankey, and Jony Ive.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of OpenAI's hardware bet, underscoring strategic commitment despite an unproven product category and now direct litigation exposure.
insights.quote: last year it paid $6.5 billion to acquire a startup called IO Products
insights.locator: And last year it paid $6.5 billion

insights.claim: Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI is likely less about monetary damages and more a strategic move to slow OpenAI's hardware ambitions and defend the iPhone's position as the primary computing platform for the AI era.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: If accurate, expect Apple to keep escalating legal and competitive pressure on any OpenAI hardware launch, since Apple views voice-first agent hardware as a threat to iPhone dominance.
insights.quote: I think what Apple actually wants is to slow down OpenAI's hardware ambitions because it's really continuing—Apple is continuing to go all in on the iPhone as the primary computing platform for the AI era.
insights.locator: My take here, and Leah, I'm curious

insights.claim: Bloomberg reporting described OpenAI's forthcoming hardware device as speaker-like with motorized elements, prompting skepticism that a neutral Apple device supporting multiple AI backends could out-compete a single-purpose OpenAI gadget.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Investor takeaway: don't overweight OpenAI's hardware optionality against Apple's ability to ship a comparable, model-agnostic speaker device.
insights.quote: if OpenAI is banking on hardware, I am skeptical. I feel like ultimately Apple can still make a speaker.
insights.locator: some reporting came out this week also about what the device is eventually going to look like

insights.claim: Litigation discovery in Apple's lawsuit against OpenAI is expected to surface internal communications between the two companies, a likely source of embarrassing disclosures for both sides.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Watch for discovery-driven news flow over the coming months that could move sentiment on both companies independent of the underlying legal merits.
insights.quote: discovery means we're going to get to read so many emails of these companies talking trash about each other and themselves
insights.locator: What I am most excited about about this whole process

insights.claim: OpenAI employees launched a rival super PAC called Guardrails Alliance with $5 million in initial funding and a $15 million fundraising target for the election cycle, explicitly positioned as a counterweight to the $100 million 'Leading the Future' fund tied to OpenAI president Greg Brockman.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Internal OpenAI dissent over AI policy is materializing as competing political spending, a signal of employee-level friction relevant to retention/culture and the broader AI-regulation lobbying fight.
insights.quote: It launched last month with $5 million in initial funding... It's going to be a counterweight to that $100 million Leading the Future fund
insights.locator: OpenAI's headaches are not just limited to the Apple lawsuit

insights.claim: A separate PAC founded by Anthropic employees, Public First Action, has raised roughly $20 million to also oppose the Leading the Future fund, meaning combined pro-guardrail funding still trails Leading the Future's $100 million by roughly 4x.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Despite two well-funded rival PACs, the pro-acceleration lobby retains a large financial edge, suggesting AI-friendly deregulatory positions keep an advantage in state and federal races absent a shift in funding balance.
insights.quote: 25 million versus 100 million, that's obviously still a massive difference

insights.claim: An outgunned political fund like Guardrails Alliance gets more impact per dollar by targeting cheap down-ballot and local races (such as in Maine) rather than expensive statewide media markets (such as Texas), since local volunteer power and radio spots there are far cheaper.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: A playbook for how underfunded advocacy money can still shift AI policy outcomes, useful for tracking which state legislative races could become AI-regulation flashpoints.
insights.quote: Radio spots in Maine versus radio spots in Austin, there are different capacities here.
insights.locator: To me, this says that they need to be working on down-ballot local campaigns

insights.claim: A $200,000 donation to Guardrails Alliance came from OpenAI research engineer Juan Felipe Cerón Uribe, who has spent years working on mitigating AI's potential societal harms, an unusually large personal political donation from a rank-and-file technical employee.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Signals genuine ideological conviction among safety-focused OpenAI staff and raises the question of internal retaliation risk at a company whose leadership leans pro-deregulation.
insights.quote: one of the largest donors was a research engineer. He gave 200K, research engineer Juan Felipe Cerón Uribe
insights.locator: Look, this is so much to me the grassroots versus the billionaire

themes.theme: DOGE's Unaccountable Use of AI in Government

insights.claim: DOGE staffers at HUD used AI tools to identify contracts and regulations for rescission or cancellation, and HUD is now denying FOIA requests about this AI use by incorrectly claiming an 'AI exemption' that does not exist under US law.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: A concrete example of opaque, unauditable AI-driven government decision-making already embedded in federal contract and housing policy, a governance risk relevant to gov-tech and public-sector AI vendors.
insights.quote: There are no laws in the US that require the government to disclose if AI has been used in the creation of rules, policies, or regulation.

insights.claim: Scott Langmack, who joined DOGE from proptech startup Kukun, is now the executive director of 'deregulation AI' at the Office of Management and Budget, showing DOGE-era AI-deregulation personnel have become permanently embedded in the executive branch rather than disappearing after DOGE's wind-down.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Institutionalization of an AI-for-deregulation function inside OMB is a durable policy vector worth tracking for future rule rescissions across other federal agencies, not just HUD.
insights.quote: he's now the executive director of deregulation AI at the Office of Management and Budget, which falls under the executive office of the president
insights.locator: The last thing I want to leave you guys with

themes.theme: New York Data Center Moratorium & National Politics

insights.claim: New York became the first state to impose a statewide moratorium on new large-scale (50MW+) data centers via a one-year executive order, pausing environmental review and proposing to end related tax incentives.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: First-mover statewide precedent other states may copy; a direct regulatory headwind for hyperscale data center siting/permitting timelines and related tax-incentive economics.
insights.quote: New York became the first state in the country to impose a statewide moratorium on building new data centers.

insights.claim: Trump publicly attacked New York's data center moratorium on Truth Social, inaccurately claiming affected projects are relocating to Alabama, Florida, Texas, and Arizona, and framing data centers as a jobs and tax-revenue issue ahead of the midterms.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Data center siting policy is becoming a national partisan flashpoint tied to the midterms, which could accelerate red states courting hyperscalers with tax incentives while blue states tighten rules.
insights.quote: One of the biggest driving forces in the future for jobs are data centers... These companies are now being sought in Alabama, Florida, Texas, Arizona, and many other states
insights.locator: I was refreshing Truth Social, convinced that Trump was going to weigh in

insights.claim: Despite Trump's partisan framing, opposition to data centers is explicitly bipartisan, with Republicans and Democrats in multiple states backing moratoriums or reform driven by local community impact such as higher utility bills and water strain.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Investors should not assume red state equals pro-data-center and blue state equals anti when modeling siting and regulatory risk state by state; local grid and water impact is the dominant driver.
insights.quote: You have Republicans and Democrats that have been opposing data centers in their communities asking for reform, asking for moratoriums.
insights.locator: The reason I was clicking refresh on Truth Social

insights.claim: Industry conversations suggest startups outside of well-capitalized players like SpaceX and OpenAI remain severely constrained by compute and data-center access, which tempers 'AI bubble' concerns because underlying demand still appears to exceed supply.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: A demand-side data point against the AI-overcapacity thesis: compute scarcity for smaller players suggests data center buildout still has room to run despite the political backlash.
insights.quote: it really surprises me how constrained startups feel in particular by compute... it still just feels like the appetite is enormous
insights.locator: Well, no, I think that that's a fair point to make

insights.claim: Restrictions on data center siting are partly justified because such projects are often approved and built with minimal advance community notice, leaving residents no input until permitting is already complete.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Supports a thesis that data center developers should expect longer, more contentious local entitlement processes going forward, raising project timeline and cost risk industry-wide.
insights.quote: these deals were made a while ago or when they do happen, you don't necessarily know until the permitting's done that this is even coming in
insights.locator: I disagree. I think the carveouts here make sense.

insights.claim: The 2026 US cyclosporiasis outbreak is the worst on record, with the CDC suspecting close to 7,000 cases nationwide, Michigan alone reporting over 3,700, versus a typical year's handful to few dozen cases nationally, with lettuce as the leading suspected source.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: A concrete food-safety event with potential read-through to leafy-greens supply chains, retailers, and consumer confidence in fresh produce.
insights.quote: The CDC suspects there may be close to 7,000 cases already nationwide... on Wednesday, their case count was just updated to more than 3,700 cases.
insights.locator: Yeah. So there's no good way to talk about this

insights.claim: Roughly a quarter of CDC staff has been cut under the current administration, which is hampering outbreak investigation capacity broadly, even though the specific surveillance system implicated (FoodNet) was not designed for real-time outbreak detection.
insights.speaker: Brian Barrett
insights.so_what: Ongoing federal public-health staffing cuts are a structural risk factor for slower detection and response to future foodborne and infectious disease outbreaks.
insights.quote: about a quarter of the CDC has been cut under this administration, and that is going to have ripple effects across all of public health
insights.locator: So a former CDC official told the Washington Post
